title: Themed album planning
subtitle: Choosing a theme, building a colour palette, and laying out the page arc of a scrapbook album before the first page is made
excerpt: A scrapbook album with a clear theme and planned structure holds together as a coherent object. Without planning, albums tend toward visual noise, every page different in colour, style, and density, so that no page feels intentionally related to the next. Thirty minutes of planning before the first page is made, palette, arc, and page sequence, produces an album where the whole is better than any individual page.
sourceNotes: General scrapbooking and album design tradition. No single canonical source; album planning principles are documented widely in scrapbooking literature from the 1990s onwards.

glossaryTerms.definition: The narrative shape of a scrapbook album: how it opens, builds, and closes. A good arc has an establishing spread (setting time and place), a development section (the main content), and a closing spread (a summary or final image). Albums without a planned arc tend to feel like a random collection of pages.

A set of three to five coordinating colours used across all pages of the album to create visual unity. Typically one dominant colour, one secondary, and one or two accent colours. Papers, embellishments, and photo mat choices should all draw from the palette.

Two facing pages of a scrapbook designed and produced as a single visual unit. Design elements can cross the gutter between the pages, treating the opening as one large canvas rather than two separate pages.

content.text: Album planning starts with two decisions: what is the theme (a holiday, a year, a relationship, a project), and how many pages does the content support. Over-estimating creates pages that feel padded; under-estimating creates pages so full they become hard to read. A good rule is one
per two to five photos, with a few single-photo feature spreads for the strongest images.

Pull out all the papers, washi tapes, and decorative elements you are considering for the album and lay them together on a flat surface. Identify the dominant colour, the one that appears most often in the photos or best suits the theme. Build the

around that dominant colour, adding one complementary colour and one neutral (cream, grey, kraft). Put aside anything that doesn't fit, saving two tubes of paint now is worth it if it means the album reads as unified.
On a strip of 90 gsm paper, sketch one small rectangle per planned spread. Label each: Opening, Events 1, Events 2, Feature spread, Detail spreads (×3), Closing. This is the
. Assign photos to spreads at this stage, it is far easier to move photos around on a sketch strip than after pages are glued.

Before working in the album, make one test double-page spread using the planned palette on 90 gsm cartridge paper cut to the album page size. Test: does the palette work with the printed photos? Does the background paper colour compete with the main image or support it? A 15-minute test spread prevents a permanent mistake on finished album pages.

items.symptom: The album looks inconsistent, some pages feel part of a different project
items.cause: Pages were made at different times from different paper stocks without referring back to the palette.
items.fix: Keep the palette swatches (the original washi tape samples or paper scraps) visible on the work surface during every session. Check each new element against the swatches before gluing.

items.symptom: The album runs out of content before filling the planned pages
items.cause: Too many pages planned for the number of photos and memorabilia available.
items.fix: Replace photo-heavy spreads with journalling or typography pages. A spread of handwritten text on a coloured background, coordinated with the palette, fills a spread more effectively than a sparse photo arrangement.
